_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Siegel et al. CA Cancer J Clin 2023 and Quaedvlieg et al. BJD 2006. Malignant transformation rates from Criscione et al. J Am Acad Dermatol 2009 (PMID 19231642). Treatment evidence from Lebwohl et al. NEJM 2003 (PMID 12525726) and AAD clinical practice guidelines 2021. 5-FU vs cryotherapy comparative data from Gupta & Paquet 2013 meta-analysis.

h1Title: Actinic Keratosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Actinic keratosis is a rough, scaly patch of skin caused by years of cumulative ultraviolet radiation exposure that produces premalignant keratinocyte dysplasia in the epidermis. Approximately 58 million Americans have at least one lesion, making it one of the most common diagnoses in dermatology; fair-skinned individuals over 40 bear the heaviest burden. Lesions typically appear on sun-exposed areas — the scalp, face, ears, forearms, and hands — as sandpaper-textured patches that may itch or bleed. Without treatment, an estimated 0.025-0.16% of individual lesions per year progress to squamous cell carcinoma of the skin. Treatments including cryotherapy, topical 5-fluorouracil, imiquimod, and photodynamic therapy are highly effective at clearing lesions and preventing malignant transformation.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 58 million people affected in the United States (American Academy of Dermatology 2021); prevalence rises to 40-60% in adults over 70 in high-UV regions such as Australia
keyStats.demographics: Fair-skinned (Fitzpatrick type I-II) individuals at highest risk; men affected more often than women (2:1) due to greater occupational sun exposure; Australian adults have among the highest rates globally
keyStats.avgAge: Most lesions appear from age 40 onward; mean age at diagnosis approximately 55-65 years in US dermatology practices
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 40 million new cases diagnosed annually worldwide; roughly 10% of cases progress to squamous cell carcinoma over a lifetime without treatment

definition:
Actinic keratosis (ICD-10: L57.0), also called solar keratosis or senile keratosis, is an intraepidermal neoplastic condition characterized by dysplastic keratinocytes in the lower layers of the epidermis caused by cumulative exposure to ultraviolet-B radiation, primarily from sunlight and tanning devices. The term 'actinic' derives from the Greek word for ray, reflecting the UV-driven etiology. Histologically, AK shows atypia of the basal keratinocytes with loss of polarity, hyperchromatism, and abnormal mitoses confined above the basement membrane — the defining feature that separates it from invasive squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), in which atypical cells breach the basement membrane and enter the dermis.

AK sits on a biological continuum with SCC; molecular studies show identical p53 mutations in both, supporting the consensus that AK represents an early, in situ stage of keratinocyte carcinoma. Whether lesions regress, persist, or progress depends on immune surveillance, continued UV exposure, and lesion thickness. Clinically, AK presents as a rough, scaly, erythematous macule or patch 1-25 mm in diameter on chronically sun-damaged skin. Specialty management is primarily by dermatologists and, for field cancerization (large confluent areas), by Mohs surgeons and photodynamic therapy specialists. Immunocompromised individuals — transplant recipients, HIV-positive patients, and those on long-term systemic steroids — develop more widespread and more rapidly progressive lesions.

typesClassification.description: Thick, warty scale overlying the lesion that is difficult to remove without bleeding. Higher keratin burden but not inherently more likely to progress; however, the scale may obscure underlying invasive change and warrants biopsy.
Thin, flat lesion with fine scale, often seen in heavily photodamaged skin; may be flesh-colored or faintly erythematous. Tends to occur in areas of diffuse field cancerization.

AK of the lower lip mucosa, presenting as scaling, fissuring, and blurring of the vermilion border. Carries a higher malignant transformation risk (approximately 10-20%) than cutaneous AK because the lip mucosa is thinner and continuously exposed.
Contains increased melanin, producing a brownish or gray color that can mimic lentigo maligna or seborrheic keratosis; dermoscopy or biopsy is usually required for confident diagnosis.

Histologically shows dysplasia throughout the full epidermal thickness, closely resembling Bowen's disease (squamous cell carcinoma in situ); carries a higher immediate malignant risk and is treated aggressively.

primarySymptoms: Rough, gritty texture on a sun-exposed skin patch — classically described as feeling like sandpaper — often more easily felt by running a fingertip across the skin than seen by eye.
Erythematous or pink-red scaly macule or plaque 1-25 mm in diameter, most commonly on the face, scalp (especially bald areas), ears, dorsal forearms, and back of the hands.
Intermittent itching, stinging, or burning sensation within the lesion, typically worsening after sun exposure or heat.
Dry, flaking, or peeling surface that regrows scale shortly after picking or rubbing off.
Thickened, hard, wart-like elevated surface in hyperkeratotic lesions, which may resemble a small cutaneous horn.
Bleeding with minimal trauma, such as rubbing a towel across the affected area.
Skin tightness or tenderness at the lesion site, particularly after sunburn or in the presence of surrounding photodamage.
Color variation ranging from flesh-toned or pink to red, brown, or gray, sometimes with a pigmented halo.
Clusters of multiple lesions in a confluent 'field' of sun-damaged skin on the same anatomical area, known as field cancerization.
earlyWarningSigns: A persistently rough patch on the face, scalp, or hands that does not heal over 4-6 weeks despite moisturizing, suggesting epidermal dysplasia rather than simple dry skin.
Recurrent peeling or crusting at the same spot on the lip, ear, or forehead that returns after being picked off.
Mild chronic soreness or tingling on a sun-exposed scalp patch in a fair-skinned adult over 40.
A newly tender or pruritic patch in an area of obvious photodamage — freckling, solar lentigines, and wrinkled, atrophic skin — which forms the background for AK development.
emergencySigns: Rapid increase in lesion size over 2-4 weeks, induration, fixation to underlying tissue, or ulceration — features suggesting transformation to invasive squamous cell carcinoma requiring urgent biopsy.
Satellite nodules, regional lymph node enlargement, or pain along a nerve distribution adjacent to a known hyperkeratotic AK — signs of early SCC with possible perineural spread.
A lesion on the lower lip that becomes indurated, bleeds easily, or develops an ulcer — actinic cheilitis with suspected SCC transformation warrants same-week dermatology review.

causes.description: UVB (290-320 nm) generates cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers and 6-4 photoproducts in keratinocyte DNA, particularly at dipyrimidine sites in the p53 tumor suppressor gene. After decades of repair failure, p53 mutations accumulate, clonal dysplastic cells expand, and AK develops. The mutation signature is identical to invasive SCC, confirming a direct biological relationship.

UVA (320-400 nm) causes indirect DNA damage via reactive oxygen species and photosensitization reactions. Indoor tanning before age 35 increases AK risk and subsequent SCC risk by approximately 75% (IARC 2007 meta-analysis). Tanning-device UVA doses can exceed peak summer sunshine intensities.

Solid-organ transplant recipients on long-term calcineurin inhibitors or azathioprine develop AK at rates 250-times higher than the general population by 10 years post-transplant; over 40% require field-treatment annually. Loss of cutaneous immune surveillance allows dysplastic keratinocytes to escape elimination.

causes.cause: Fair skin phenotype and deficient melanin protection
causes.description: Fitzpatrick skin type I-II individuals produce predominantly pheomelanin, which absorbs less UV energy and generates more oxidative stress than eumelanin-dominant types. Red or blonde hair (MC1R variants), blue eyes, and propensity to burn rather than tan collectively lower the UV dose threshold for AK development by an estimated 10-30 fold.

causes.cause: Occupational and recreational cumulative UV exposure
causes.description: Outdoor workers accumulate 3-10 times more annual UV dose than office workers. Farmers, roofers, sailors, fishermen, and construction workers have AK prevalence rates of 40-80% after age 50. Proximity to the equator, high altitude, reflective surfaces (water, snow, sand), and absence of shade amplify occupational UV dose.

Beta-papillomavirus types (especially HPV 5 and 8) are detected in a subset of AK and SCC lesions and may suppress UV-damage-induced apoptosis, promoting survival of dysplastic clones. The role is particularly pronounced in immunosuppressed transplant recipients where beta-HPV prevalence exceeds 80%.

riskFactors.description: Fair-skinned individuals with red or blonde hair and tendency to burn rather than tan have the highest lifetime AK risk; prevalence exceeds 60% in Australian men over 70 with skin type I.
AK prevalence rises steeply with age; decade-by-decade cumulative UV damage exceeds epidermal repair capacity. By age 70, over 25% of fair-skinned Americans have at least one lesion.
Outdoor occupations and sun-seeking behavior account for the greatest modifiable risk. Each additional decade of high UV exposure raises AK risk approximately 1.5-fold.
Tanning device use before age 35 increases SCC risk 75% (IARC meta-analysis) and AK risk proportionately; each tanning session adds to the DNA mutation burden.

Organ transplant recipients on calcineurin inhibitors develop AK 250-fold more often than the general population; switching to mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus) reduces AK incidence by approximately 50% in transplant cohorts.

Populations in Australia, New Zealand, and sunbelt US states (Florida, Arizona) have AK prevalence rates 2-3 times higher than northern European counterparts at equivalent ages. High altitude adds a further 4-10% UV dose increase per 300 m elevation.

riskFactors.factor: Previous non-melanoma skin cancer or AK history
riskFactors.description: Patients with a prior SCC have a 30-50% chance of developing another SCC within 5 years; prior AK treatment doubles the likelihood of finding new AK on annual examination.

affectedDemographics: Australian adults: prevalence rates of 40-60% in those over 70, the highest globally, driven by high ambient UV, outdoor culture, and a predominantly fair-skinned population.
US adults: an estimated 58 million affected, with highest rates in southern states; over 4.9 million physician visits annually for AK diagnosis and treatment (AAD 2021).
Men over women by approximately 2:1 in most population studies, attributed to greater occupational sun exposure and lower rates of photoprotection behavior.
Immunosuppressed populations: solid-organ transplant recipients develop AK at epidemic rates — roughly 40-80% within 10 years of transplant, with SCC as the most common post-transplant cancer.
diagnosisOverview:
Actinic keratosis is diagnosed clinically in the majority of cases. Dermatoscopy (dermoscopy) enhances clinical accuracy, particularly for pigmented or flat lesions that overlap with lentigo maligna or seborrheic keratosis. On dermoscopy, AK shows a strawberry pattern — erythema with follicular openings, white-yellowish scale, and a pseudonetwork structure around hair follicles.

Biopsy is reserved for lesions with atypical features: rapid growth, induration, ulceration, fixation to underlying tissue, hypertrophic thickening resistant to cryotherapy, or those on the lip. A shave biopsy or punch biopsy provides adequate tissue to confirm dysplasia grade, assess for basement membrane invasion (SCC), and evaluate perineural spread. Full-thickness punch biopsy is preferred for lesions on or near the lip.

Dermatologists also assess for field cancerization — a zone of subclinical UV-damaged skin surrounding visible AK, visible on reflectance confocal microscopy and relevant to field-directed therapy selection. The number and location of lesions influence treatment choice: isolated lesions favor cryotherapy, whereas multiple confluent lesions on the scalp or face favor field treatment with 5-fluorouracil cream, ingenol mebutate gel, photodynamic therapy, or imiquimod. Reflectance confocal microscopy and optical coherence tomography are emerging non-invasive tools in specialist centers for evaluating borderline lesions without biopsy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies AK by morphology and differentiates from SCC, melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and lentigo maligna
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The dermatologist uses a handheld lighted magnifier placed on the skin. No pain or preparation required. Takes 5-10 minutes. The strawberry pattern and follicular keratosis are key dermoscopic hallmarks of AK.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms AK histologically, rules out invasive SCC, and grades dysplasia (mild, moderate, severe)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The skin is cleaned and a small injection of local anesthetic is given; after 30 seconds the area is numb. The dermatologist uses a blade to shave the surface lesion into a pot of formalin. Slight pressure is applied. The wound heals in 1-2 weeks. Results return in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Obtains full-thickness skin for suspected SCC or lesions on the lip where complete epidermal assessment is required
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anesthetic injection, then a 3-4 mm circular punch tool removes a core of skin. One or two sutures are placed. The site heals in 1-2 weeks. Histopathology results in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive imaging of individual cells and architectural features within the epidermis and superficial dermis to differentiate AK from early SCC without biopsy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe is gently applied to the skin surface for 10-20 minutes. No pain, no needle. Used primarily in specialist melanoma and skin cancer centers. Results interpreted immediately by a trained dermato-oncologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Highlights the extent of photodamage and subclinical field cancerization under UV-A light, particularly on the face and scalp
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld UV lamp is used in a darkened room. No discomfort. Actinic damage fluoresces as pale or mottled areas against normal skin's uniform fluorescence. Takes under 5 minutes.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment selection depends on the number of lesions, their location, and whether field cancerization is present. American Academy of Dermatology (AAD) guidelines and the European Dermatology Forum (EDF) consensus 2019 both recommend a tiered approach: lesion-directed therapy for isolated lesions and field-directed therapy for multiple lesions or clinically apparent field cancerization.

Cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen is the most widely used lesion-directed treatment, applied for 10-30 seconds to achieve a 1-2 mm freeze margin beyond the visible lesion. Complete response rates of 67-85% per lesion have been reported in randomized controlled trials, with re-treatment needed for residual or recurrent lesions.

Field-directed topical therapies include 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) 5% cream applied twice daily for 2-4 weeks, which achieves complete clearance in 38-53% of patients and significant partial response in up to 90%; imiquimod 5% cream three times per week for 4-16 weeks (complete response 50-57%); diclofenac sodium 3% gel twice daily for 90 days; and tirbanibulin 1% ointment once daily for 5 days.

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) using aminolevulinic acid (ALA) or methyl aminolevulinate (MAL) activated by red or blue light provides excellent cosmetic results and complete response rates of 70-91% in head and neck AK in randomized trials, making it preferred for cosmetically sensitive areas.

For immunocompromised patients — particularly transplant recipients — field surveillance every 3-6 months, combination approaches with 5-FU plus cryotherapy, oral retinoids (acitretin 25-50 mg/day), and considering switch from calcineurin inhibitors to mTOR inhibitors form the backbone of management. Nicotinamide (vitamin B3) 500 mg twice daily reduces new AK and non-melanoma skin cancer rates by 23% over 12 months in high-risk patients in the ONTRAC randomized trial.

medicalTreatments.name: Cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen
medicalTreatments.description: Liquid nitrogen applied by spray or cotton-tipped applicator for 10-30 seconds, producing controlled local skin necrosis and selective destruction of dysplastic keratinocytes. Single freeze-thaw cycle standard; double freeze-thaw for hyperkeratotic lesions. Mild transient pain, blistering, and redness follow.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete response per lesion 67-85% in RCTs; recurrence rate approximately 5-15% at 12 months (Gupta & Paquet 2013 meta-analysis)

medicalTreatments.name: 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) 5% cream (apply twice daily for 2-4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Topical antimetabolite that blocks thymidylate synthetase, selectively targeting rapidly dividing dysplastic keratinocytes. Produces visible erosion and inflammation of treated fields; considered a marker of treatment adequacy. Avoids eyes and mucous membranes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete clearance 38-53%; partial clearance up to 90%; field recurrence at 1 year approximately 25% (Lebwohl et al. NEJM 2003)

medicalTreatments.name: Imiquimod 5% cream (three times per week for 4-16 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Toll-like receptor 7/8 agonist that stimulates innate and adaptive immunity to eliminate dysplastic keratinocytes. Less irritating erosion profile than 5-FU but slower course. Flu-like local reactions are expected. Particularly useful in cosmetically sensitive areas.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete clearance 50-57% at end of treatment; sustained clearance at 1 year approximately 45% in RCTs

medicalTreatments.name: Aminolevulinic acid-photodynamic therapy (ALA-PDT)
medicalTreatments.description: Topical ALA prodrug is applied to the field for 1-3 hours, absorbed preferentially by dysplastic cells, and converted to protoporphyrin IX. Subsequent activation by red or blue light (light dose 37 J/cm²) generates reactive oxygen species that destroy the abnormal cells. Excellent cosmesis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete clearance 70-91% for face and scalp AK in meta-analyses; recurrence rate 25-50% at 2 years, requiring re-treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Tirbanibulin 1% ointment (once daily for 5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Dual-mechanism tubulin polymerization inhibitor and Src kinase inhibitor approved by FDA in 2020. Short 5-day course with mild-to-moderate local skin reactions. Suitable for single fields up to 25 cm² on the face or scalp.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete clearance 44-54% vs 5% vehicle (placebo) at day 57 in pivotal phase III trials

medicalTreatments.name: Nicotinamide (vitamin B3) 500 mg twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: Oral precursor to NAD+ that enhances DNA repair and reduces UV-induced immunosuppression. Used as adjuvant chemoprevention rather than primary treatment. Safe, inexpensive, and available over the counter.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 23% reduction in new AK formation and new non-melanoma skin cancers over 12 months vs placebo in ONTRAC RCT (Chen et al. NEJM 2015)

surgicalOptions.name: Shave excision or curettage
surgicalOptions.description: Scraping or shaving the lesion under local anesthetic. Reserved for thick hyperkeratotic AK resistant to cryotherapy and for symptomatic lesions on the scalp where biopsy is simultaneously needed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Clearance rate near 95% when lesion is fully curetted; primary purpose is diagnosis of invasion rather than field control

surgicalOptions.name: Mohs micrographic surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Used only when biopsy has confirmed invasive SCC arising within an AK, not for AK itself. 5-year local recurrence rates of 3-5% for primary SCC in situ (Bowen) vs 1-3% for non-aggressive invasive SCC.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year local cure rate 97-99% for low-risk SCC; gold standard for SCC on the face and ears

alternativeTreatments.name: Green tea polyphenols (EGCG topical)
alternativeTreatments.description: Epigallocatechin gallate has shown suppression of UV-induced AK in small phase II trials; not currently approved but studied in high-risk transplant populations.

alternativeTreatments.name: Retinoids (acitretin 25 mg/day)
alternativeTreatments.description: Oral acitretin reduces AK burden and SCC rate in solid-organ transplant recipients at high risk; a 2018 Cochrane review found consistent though modest evidence for chemoprevention in immunosuppressed patients.

whySeeSpecialist: Dermatologist evaluation is warranted for any rough, scaly, or bleeding patch that does not resolve within 4-6 weeks on sun-exposed skin, particularly in adults over 40 with a history of prolonged sun exposure, outdoor occupational history, or prior skin cancer. Prompt referral is essential for lesions on the lip, rapidly enlarging lesions, or those that recur despite a full course of topical therapy, as these features increase the probability of invasive SCC.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a board-certified dermatologist with experience in skin cancer prevention and a dermoscope, or a dermatologist who offers photodynamic therapy for field cancerization. Immunocompromised patients — especially solid-organ transplant recipients — should be managed in a high-risk skin cancer clinic with joint dermatology-transplant oversight and access to reflectance confocal microscopy.
hospitalCriteria: Access to dermatology with photodynamic therapy equipment and dermoscopy
Histopathology laboratory for same-day or next-day biopsy results
Mohs surgical unit for confirmed invasive SCC arising in or near AK
keyFacilities: Dermatology outpatient clinics with photodynamic therapy
High-risk skin cancer clinics at academic medical centers
Mohs surgery practices for confirmed invasive carcinoma
preventionStrategies: Apply broad-spectrum SPF 30 or higher sunscreen to all exposed skin daily, even on cloudy days; reapply every 2 hours during outdoor activity. Daily sunscreen reduces new AK formation by 24% in randomized trials.
Wear UV-protective clothing (UPF 50+), wide-brimmed hats, and UV-blocking sunglasses during peak UV hours (10 a.m. to 4 p.m.).
Seek shade and limit direct sun exposure between 10 a.m. and 4 p.m., especially at high altitudes or near reflective surfaces such as snow, water, or sand.
Avoid indoor tanning beds entirely; each session adds to cumulative UV mutation load with no safe dose threshold.
Take nicotinamide 500 mg twice daily if you are at high risk (over 60, fair-skinned, prior AK or skin cancer) — the ONTRAC trial showed a 23% reduction in new skin cancers over 12 months.
Attend annual full-body skin checks with a dermatologist if you have a history of AK, prior skin cancer, outdoor occupation, or immunosuppression.
lifestyleModifications: Apply sunscreen every morning as the last step of a skincare routine, including on the scalp, ears, and back of hands — the sites where AK is most commonly overlooked.
Perform monthly self-skin checks of sun-exposed areas and note any new rough, scaly, or persistent patches.
Wear a broad-brimmed hat and long sleeves when gardening, driving long distances, or spending prolonged time outdoors.
Moisturize treated skin daily during and after field therapy courses with 5-FU or imiquimod to reduce irritation and improve treatment completion rates.
Minimize alcohol consumption, which impairs cutaneous immune surveillance and has been associated with modestly increased skin cancer risk in observational studies.
Communicate all prior AK or skin cancer history to any new physician, including GP, surgeon, and transplant team, to ensure skin surveillance is maintained.

recommended: High-antioxidant foods — blueberries, spinach, tomatoes — to support epidermal defense against UV oxidative stress
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish or flaxseed, which in a randomized trial reduced new AK in high-risk individuals by 20% at 6 months
Green tea for topical or dietary EGCG, which shows UV-protective effects in small clinical studies
A balanced diet sufficient in vitamins C, E, and beta-carotene to maintain antioxidant capacity of photodamaged skin
avoid: Excessive alcohol, associated with modestly increased non-melanoma skin cancer risk in meta-analyses
High-calorie refined diets that promote systemic inflammation, which may impair epidermal DNA repair
Supplements with beta-carotene at high dose (above 20 mg/day), as high-dose supplementation increased lung cancer risk in smokers in CARET and ATBC trials

exerciseGuidelines: Regular physical activity is beneficial for immune function and not directly contraindicated in AK. Exercise outdoors should be performed with full photoprotection — sunscreen, UV-protective clothing, and hats — because cardiovascular exercise typically occurs during higher-UV daytime hours and increases facial surface area exposed through sweating and flushing.
dailyManagement: Apply prescribed topical therapy (5-FU or imiquimod) at the correct frequency and to the full treatment field, not just visible lesions, to eradicate subclinical dysplasia.
Use a gentle moisturizer twice daily during topical treatment to manage erosion, redness, and crusting without discontinuing therapy prematurely.
Apply SPF 30+ sunscreen every morning and reapply during daytime sun exposure; photoprotection is treatment, not optional.
Keep a personal skin cancer diary noting lesion locations, treatment dates, and response, to share at dermatology review.
Avoid picking or scrubbing treated lesions, as this increases infection risk and may displace dysplastic cells.
prognosis: The prognosis for actinic keratosis is excellent when lesions are identified and treated early. The individual lesion malignant transformation rate is approximately 0.025-0.16% per year, meaning that over a decade, most individual lesions either regress spontaneously (in up to 26% of cases at 1 year) or remain stable rather than progressing. However, patients with multiple AK have a lifetime SCC risk of 6-10% and a substantially elevated risk of basal cell carcinoma; the more lesions present at any one time, the higher the cumulative transformation risk. Complete clearance with any approved therapy reduces SCC incidence in the treated field. Regular annual dermatology surveillance, consistent photoprotection, and compliance with field-directed treatment courses reduce the risk of SCC. Patients who develop SCC from AK generally present at an early stage because of existing skin cancer awareness; 5-year disease-specific survival for localized SCC exceeds 95%.
recoveryTimeline: Cryotherapy: crusting and blistering over 7-14 days; full healing in 2-4 weeks. 5-FU cream: peak inflammation at 2-3 weeks of treatment, healing within 2-4 weeks after completion. PDT: local redness and crusting over 7-14 days post-illumination; cosmetic improvement continues for 8-12 weeks. Annual or biannual surveillance maintained indefinitely given ongoing UV damage and risk of new lesions.
complications: Progression to invasive squamous cell carcinoma of the skin — risk per lesion is 0.025-0.16% per year but rises substantially with large numbers of lesions, immunosuppression, and lesions on the lip; detectable early by rapid enlargement, induration, or ulceration.
Actinic cheilitis transforming to SCC of the lip — reported malignant transformation rate of 10-20% over a lifetime; new induration or ulceration on the vermilion border warrants biopsy.
Post-treatment dyspigmentation, particularly hypopigmentation after aggressive cryotherapy on darker skin tones; less cosmetically significant with PDT or imiquimod in most patients.
Treatment-site infection: erosive fields during 5-FU or imiquimod courses are susceptible to secondary bacterial or herpes simplex infection; monitor for purulent exudate or vesicular eruption.

Psychological distress and skin cancer anxiety, particularly in patients requiring repeated field treatments; validated in surveys of transplant recipients where skin cancer vigilance affects quality of life measurably.
Inadequate treatment or lost-to-follow-up scenarios allowing undetected SCC to develop metastatic potential — annual skin surveillance mitigates this risk substantially.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Actinic Keratosis Resource
supportResources.description: Patient-facing AAD page covering recognition, treatment, and when to see a dermatologist.

supportResources.name: Skin Cancer Foundation — Actinic Keratosis
supportResources.description: Educational resource on AK recognition, sun protection, and treatment options with clinical photographs.

supportResources.name: Cancer Research UK — Actinic Keratosis
supportResources.description: UK-based information on AK, its relationship to SCC, and treatment pathways under the NHS.

confusedWithConditions.name: Basal cell carcinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: BCC is a malignant tumor with pearlescent rolled edges, telangiectasia, and central ulceration or pigmentation; AK is a scaly erythematous plaque without these features. BCC never arises from AK and does not show the rough sandpaper texture of AK. Dermoscopy and biopsy readily distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Melanoma in situ (lentigo maligna)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lentigo maligna appears as an irregular, slowly enlarging pigmented patch on the face of elderly individuals and can mimic pigmented AK. Dermoscopy shows an asymmetric pigmented follicular pattern distinct from AK's strawberry pattern. Biopsy is often required for definitive differentiation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriatic plaques are silvery, well-demarcated, symmetric, and not confined to sun-exposed areas; they appear on elbows, knees, and scalp with a different scale quality. AK is rough, isolated, and arises in photodamaged skin without the systemic or family history context typical of psoriasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema (atopic dermatitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema causes weeping, crusting, and pruritic patches in flexural areas in patients with atopic history; it occurs on any body surface. AK is localized to sun-damaged areas, produces a gritty texture rather than vesicles, and is not associated with atopy. Eczema typically responds to topical corticosteroids, whereas AK does not.

relatedConditions.name: Basal cell carcinoma
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common skin malignancy, sharing the same UV-damage etiology and often occurring in the same photodamaged skin field as AK.

relatedConditions.name: Melanoma in situ
relatedConditions.relevance: Another UV-driven epidermal precancer that occurs in the same sun-damaged population and must be differentiated from pigmented AK.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Patients on systemic psoriasis treatments (methotrexate, cyclosporine, biologics) may have modified immune responses that affect AK development and treatment response.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: HIV-related immunosuppression promotes more rapid and widespread AK and SCC development, particularly in patients with low CD4 counts on older antiretroviral regimens.

faqs.question: What does actinic keratosis look like?
faqs.answer: Actinic keratosis appears as a rough, scaly patch 1-25 mm across on sun-exposed skin. Color ranges from flesh-toned to pink, red, or brown. The surface feels like sandpaper. It most commonly affects the face, scalp, ears, and backs of the hands.

faqs.question: Is actinic keratosis the same as skin cancer?
faqs.answer: Actinic keratosis is a precancer, not an established cancer. Dysplastic cells are confined above the skin's basement membrane. Each individual lesion has a 0.025-0.16% annual chance of progressing to squamous cell carcinoma. Prompt treatment eliminates this risk.

faqs.question: What causes actinic keratosis?
faqs.answer: Cumulative exposure to ultraviolet-B radiation — primarily from sunlight over decades — causes DNA mutations in skin cells (keratinocytes), particularly in the p53 gene. Indoor tanning and immunosuppressive medication also cause AK by the same UV-damage mechanism.

faqs.question: How is actinic keratosis treated?
faqs.answer: Single lesions are most often treated with cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen), which clears 67-85% of lesions. Multiple lesions are treated with field therapies including 5-fluorouracil cream, imiquimod, or photodynamic therapy. Treatment choice depends on lesion number, location, and skin type.

faqs.question: Can actinic keratosis go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Yes. Studies show up to 26% of individual AK lesions regress spontaneously within 12 months. However, new lesions often appear in the same field, and the cumulative cancer risk increases with the total number of lesions. Dermatologists generally recommend treating all visible lesions.

faqs.question: Does actinic keratosis hurt?
faqs.answer: Lesions can itch, sting, burn, or become tender, especially after sun exposure. Some people describe a prickling sensation. Lesions may bleed with minimal friction. Many are asymptomatic and noticed only on examination.

faqs.question: How long does 5-FU cream treatment take?
faqs.answer: Standard 5-fluorouracil 5% cream is applied twice daily for 2-4 weeks. Significant redness, erosion, and crusting are expected and confirm the drug is working. Complete healing after stopping treatment takes a further 2-4 weeks. Results are reviewed 4-8 weeks after course completion.

faqs.question: Can actinic keratosis spread to other people?
faqs.answer: No. Actinic keratosis is not contagious. It results from cumulative personal UV exposure and cannot be transmitted from person to person by touch, proximity, or any other route.

faqs.question: Who gets actinic keratosis most often?
faqs.answer: Fair-skinned adults over 40 with a history of high sun exposure — outdoor workers, farmers, sailors, and those who have lived in high-UV regions — are most affected. Organ transplant recipients on immunosuppressants develop lesions at 250 times the normal rate.

faqs.question: How do doctors diagnose actinic keratosis?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is usually clinical, aided by dermoscopy. The dermatologist examines the lesion's texture, color, and dermoscopic features. A biopsy is done if the lesion is thick, rapidly growing, ulcerated, or on the lip to exclude invasive squamous cell carcinoma.

faqs.question: Does sunscreen prevent actinic keratosis?
faqs.answer: Daily broad-spectrum SPF 30 or higher sunscreen reduces new AK formation by approximately 24% in randomized trials. It cannot reverse existing UV damage but slows new lesion development when applied consistently to all sun-exposed skin, especially the face, scalp, and hands.

faqs.question: What happens if actinic keratosis is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Most individual lesions persist or regress. The cumulative annual risk of SCC progression per lesion is 0.025-0.16%. Patients with many lesions have a 6-10% lifetime SCC risk. Untreated AK on the lip carries a 10-20% lifetime malignant transformation risk.

faqs.question: Is nicotinamide useful for actinic keratosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Oral nicotinamide (vitamin B3) 500 mg twice daily reduced new AK by a statistically significant margin and cut new skin cancer incidence by 23% versus placebo over 12 months in the ONTRAC randomized trial. It is recommended for high-risk patients.

faqs.question: Can children develop actinic keratosis?
faqs.answer: Actinic keratosis is extremely rare in children. It requires decades of cumulative UV damage to develop. Children with xeroderma pigmentosum — a rare genetic DNA-repair disorder — are a notable exception and may develop AK and SCC in early childhood.

faqs.question: What is field cancerization in actinic keratosis?
faqs.answer: Field cancerization describes a broad zone of subclinical UV-damaged skin surrounding visible AK. Multiple clones of p53-mutated cells populate the field. Treating only visible lesions leaves the field at risk. Field-directed therapies like 5-FU or photodynamic therapy treat the entire zone.

metaTitle: Actinic Keratosis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Actinic keratosis: rough, scaly skin patches caused by sun damage that can progress to skin cancer. Causes, diagnosis, and treatment reviewed here.
